entities.name: saṃjñāśabda
entities.cite: the first case-ending is prescribed after the name-word

entities.name: artha
entities.cite: the name-word, at the moment of definition, has its meaning (its own form)

entities.name: prathamā
entities.cite: the first case-ending is prescribed after the name-word

axioms.name: arthavattvat
axioms.cite: the name-word, at the moment of definition, has its meaning (its own form)

axioms.name: karana
axioms.cite: Because it possesses the meaning, the first case-ending is prescribed after the name-word

axioms.name: janyate
axioms.cite: it arises tadarthat eva, out of that same meaning

denials.name: viruddha
denials.cite: The genitive construal, asya iti, 'of this', is not thereby lost

claims.name: karana
claims.cite: the first case-ending is prescribed after the name-word

rejected_readings.label: cases_contradictory
rejected_readings.rendering: The nominative and genitive case-endings express contradictory functions in the grammar and cannot both apply to the name-word.
rejected_readings.why: Asserts that nominative and genitive are mutually contradictory (viruddha), which the commentary explicitly rejects by saying the genitive 'is not thereby lost' and both express two moments of one act. This contradicts the denial that the cases contradict.

rejected_readings.label: meaning_disconnected_from_cases
rejected_readings.rendering: The name-word possesses meaning, but this fact is independent of which case-ending is prescribed in the grammar.
rejected_readings.why: Severs the connection between possession of meaning (arthavattvat) and the justification of case-endings. The axioms require that the cases arise from artha, not from some independent grammar_rule. This reading makes claims about grammar_rule that are not licensed by the axioms about artha.

notes: The verse formalizes why both nominative and genitive are valid in the defining rule: both arise from a single source—the name-word's possession of meaning (arthavattvat). The nominative expresses the name in its independent standing; the genitive expresses the name as property of the named (once assignment relates them). The grammar's tolerance of both is not lax but exact, reflecting the two logical moments of designation.
